scene_title: London. Before a tavern in Eastcheap

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: Well met, Corporal Nym.

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: Good morrow, Lieutenant Bardolph.

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: What, are Ancient Pistol and you friends yet?

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: For my part, I care not: I say little; but when time

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: shall serve, there shall be smiles; but that shall be as it

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: may. I dare not fight; but I will wink and hold out

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: mine iron: it is a simple one; but what though? it will

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: toast cheese, and it will endure cold as another man’s sword will:

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: and there’s an end.

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: I will bestow a breakfast to make you friends; and we’ll be

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: all three sworn brothers to France: let it be so, good Corporal

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: Nym.

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: Faith, I will live so long as I may, that’s the certain

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: of it; and when I cannot live any longer, I will do

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: as I may: that is my rest, that is the rendezvous of

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: it.

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: It is certain, corporal, that he is married to Nell Quickly; and

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: certainly she did you wrong; for you were troth-plight to her.

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: I cannot tell: things must be as they may: men may sleep,

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: and they may have their throats about them at that time; and

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: some say knives have edges. It must be as it may: though

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: patience be a tired mare, yet she will plod. There must be

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: conclusions. Well, I cannot tell.

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: Here comes Ancient Pistol and his wife: good corporal, be patient here.

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: How now, mine host Pistol!

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Base tike, call’st thou me host?

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Now, by this hand, I swear, I scorn the term;

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Nor shall my Nell keep lodgers.

lines.speaker: HOSTESS
lines.text: No, by my troth, not long; for we cannot lodge and board

lines.speaker: HOSTESS
lines.text: a dozen or fourteen gentlewomen that live honestly by the prick of

lines.speaker: HOSTESS
lines.text: their needles, but it will be thought we keep a bawdy house

lines.speaker: HOSTESS
lines.text: straight. Nym and Pistol draw. O well a day, Lady, if he

lines.speaker: HOSTESS
lines.text: be not drawn now! we shall see wilful adultery and murder committed.

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: Good lieutenant! good corporal! offer nothing here.

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: Pish!

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Pish for thee, Iceland dog! thou prick-ear’d cur of Iceland!

lines.speaker: HOSTESS
lines.text: Good Corporal Nym, show thy valour, and put up your sword.

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: Will you shog off? I would have you solus.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: “Solus,” egregious dog! O viper vile!

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: The “solus” in thy most mervailous face;

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: The “solus” in thy teeth, and in thy throat,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And in thy hateful lungs, yea, in thy maw, perdy,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And, which is worse, within thy nasty mouth!

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: I do retort the “solus” in thy bowels;

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: For I can take, and Pistol’s cock is up,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And flashing fire will follow.

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: I am not Barbason; you cannot conjure me. I have an humour

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: to knock you indifferently well. If you grow foul with me, Pistol,

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: I will scour you with my rapier, as I may, in fair

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: terms: if you would walk off, I would prick your guts a

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: little, in good terms, as I may: and that’s the humour of

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: it.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: O braggart vile and damned furious wight!

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: The grave doth gape, and doting death is near;

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Therefore exhale.

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: Hear me, hear me what I say: he that strikes the first

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: stroke, I’ll run him up to the hilts, as I am a

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: soldier.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: An oath of mickle might; and fury shall abate.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Give me thy fist, thy fore-foot to me give:

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Thy spirits are most tall.

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: I will cut thy throat, one time or other, in fair terms:

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: that is the humour of it.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: “Couple a gorge!”

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: That is the word. I thee defy again.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: O hound of Crete, think’st thou my spouse to get?

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: No; to the spital go,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And from the powdering-tub of infamy

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Fetch forth the lazar kite of Cressid’s kind,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Doll Tearsheet she by name, and her espouse:

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: I have, and I will hold, the quondam Quickly

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: For the only she; and—pauca, there’s enough.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Go to.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: Mine host Pistol, you must come to my master, and you, hostess:

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: he is very sick, and would to bed. Good Bardolph, put thy

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: face between his sheets, and do the office of a warming-pan. Faith,

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: he’s very ill.

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: Away, you rogue!

lines.speaker: HOSTESS
lines.text: By my troth, he’ll yield the crow a pudding one of these

lines.speaker: HOSTESS
lines.text: days. The king has killed his heart. Good husband, come home presently.

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: Come, shall I make you two friends? We must to France together:

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: why the devil should we keep knives to cut one another’s throats?

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Let floods o’erswell, and fiends for food howl on!

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: You’ll pay me the eight shillings I won of you at betting?

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Base is the slave that pays.

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: That now I will have: that’s the humour of it.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: As manhood shall compound: push home.They draw.

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: By this sword, he that makes the first thrust, I’ll kill him;

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: by this sword, I will.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Sword is an oath, and oaths must have their course.

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: Corporal Nym, and thou wilt be friends, be friends: an thou wilt

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: not, why, then, be enemies with me too. Prithee, put up.

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: I shall have my eight shillings I won from you at betting?

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: A noble shalt thou have, and present pay;

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And liquor likewise will I give to thee,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And friendship shall combine, and brotherhood:

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: I’ll live by Nym, and Nym shall live by me;

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Is not this just? for I shall sutler be

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Unto the camp, and profits will accrue.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Give me thy hand.

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: I shall have my noble?

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: In cash most justly paid.

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: Well, then, that’s the humour of’t.

lines.speaker: HOSTESS
lines.text: As ever you come of women, come in quickly to Sir John.

lines.speaker: HOSTESS
lines.text: Ah, poor heart! he is so shaked of a burning quotidian tertian,

lines.speaker: HOSTESS
lines.text: that it is most lamentable to behold. Sweet men, come to him.

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: The king hath run bad humours on the knight; that’s the even of it.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Nym, thou hast spoke the right;

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: His heart is fracted and corroborate.

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: The king is a good king: but it must be as it

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: may; he passes some humours and careers.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Let us condole the knight; for, lambkins, we will live.

stage_directions.text: Enter Corporal Nym and Lieutenant Bardolph.

glosses.definition: 'Nym' means 'to take/filch' in Elizabethan slang — his name is a joke about petty theft
'Ancient' = ensign, the lowest commissioned officer; Pistol's name signals noisy bombast
shut my eyes
keep my sword out — Nym's comic threat
the sword is fit only for toasting cheese — a pathetic weapon
men joined by oath in military fellowship
my decision; my settled resolve — gambling term, 'the stakes I reserve'
ultimate destination; final resort
the Hostess of the Boar's Head tavern, familiar from the Henry IV plays
betrothed; formally engaged by oath

glosses.phrase: patience be a tired mare, yet she will plod
glosses.definition: Nym's proverbial locution: even an exhausted horse keeps going
my innkeeper — Bardolph's familiar address insults Pistol's new domestic role
low-bred cur; contemptible dog
run a brothel — the Hostess's anxious euphemism
with sword drawn
expression of contempt — a hiss of dismissal
a breed of long-haired lap-dog — Pistol insults Nym as a yappy, effete cur
sharp-eared mongrel
clear off; buzz off — Nym's low slang
Latin: 'alone' — Nym wants a private duel; Pistol mishears it as an insult
outstandingly bad; conspicuous in wickedness
marvellous — Pistol's inflated diction
stomach; gullet
by God (par Dieu) — mild oath
hurl the word back into Pistol's guts — Pistolian bombast
my pistol's cocked and ready to fire — with bawdy double-meaning Pistol cultivates
a demon's name from contemporary demonology — Nym refuses to be 'conjured' like a devil
Nym's catchphrase — 'mood', 'inclination'; he over-uses it comically throughout the scene
run you through with my sword — 'scour' = cleanse by piercing
creature; fellow (archaic)
draw (your sword) — Pistol's Latinate way of saying 'unsheathe'
great; much (archaic/dialect)
my anger will lessen
Pistol's butchery of the French 'couper la gorge' — 'cut the throat'
mock-epic insult — Cretan hounds were proverbially fierce
hospital — especially one for the diseased poor, often the pox
the sweating-tub used to treat venereal disease; by extension, a house of ill repute
leprous beggar; diseased wretch
descendants of Cressida — who in Elizabethan tradition ends as a leprous beggar; slang for syphilitic women
a notorious prostitute from Henry IV Part 2 — Pistol re-names the Hostess into her world
the former Quickly — Latin 'quondam' = formerly; she is now Pistol's wife
Latin: 'few (words)' — enough said
a long-handled pan of hot coals to warm a bed — the Boy's crude joke about Bardolph's face
give the crow his meal — i.e. die, so the crows can feed on him
the debt Pistol owes Nym from a wager — the quarrel's actual trigger

glosses.phrase: Base is the slave that pays
glosses.definition: Pistol's motto: only slaves pay their debts; gentlemen don't
let our manhood settle it; let swordplay decide
a gold coin worth roughly six shillings eightpence
army camp-follower who sells food and drink to soldiers
the Hostess's malapropism — 'quotidian' (daily fever) and 'tertian' (every third day) are contradictory; she means he has a raging fever
broken and corroborated (confirmed) — Pistol means 'broken'; he doesn't know what 'corroborate' means
gives vent to some moods and outbursts — Nym jargon; 'careers' = runs
sympathize with; mourn for
little lambs — Pistol's incongruously tender address

annotations.title: The other England — Eastcheap after Falstaff
annotations.body: The Eastcheap scene that opens Act II introduces the remnants of Falstaff's old company — Bardolph, Nym, Pistol — and installs them as the play's recurring comic counterpoint to the official war. Their world is petty theft, doubtful marriages, and sword-quarrels about eight-shilling debts. Shakespeare is doing something structurally specific here: he is showing us who Henry's war actually sweeps up. The Chorus has just named England's 'youth on fire' and 'the mirror of all Christian kings'; the scene immediately undercuts the image by staging Nym's plodding cowardice and Pistol's inflated bombast as the mobilization's reality on the ground. The contrast is not sentimental — Shakespeare is not asking us to love Pistol — but it is structural: the play's official voice and its low-comic undervoice alternate throughout, and this is the first such alternation.
sources: C. L. Barber, Shakespeare's Festive Comedy (1959)
Robert Weimann, Shakespeare and the Popular Tradition in the Theater (1978)

annotations.title: Pistol's diction — mock-heroic as a character
annotations.body: Pistol is Shakespeare's most fully realized parody of a certain kind of Elizabethan actor — the roaring, classically-allusive, stage-villain whose speech is studded with half-digested Latin, misquoted classical mythology, and oaths designed to sound imposing. His vocabulary is a running pastiche: 'Couple a gorge' (bungled French), 'quondam Quickly' (Latinate pretension), 'pauca' (schoolboy Latin), 'Cressid's kind' (misused Troy-matter). His rhythmic, near-blank-verse inflections parody the high style of earlier Elizabethan drama (especially Marlowe). The comedy depends on our hearing the rhetorical mode Pistol is imitating. Importantly, Pistol's inflation is not just funny: it is a window into what soldier-theatre could sound like in 1599, and the play will later (5.1) strip away the inflated pose entirely, showing us the ordinary scrounger underneath.
sources: Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)

trials.prompt: What is the structural function of the Eastcheap scene in Act II?
options: To introduce new characters for the wooing subplot
To provide comic relief with no thematic content
To show the actual human material of Henry's 'youth of England on fire' — low-comic scroungers preparing for pillage — as a counterpoint to the Chorus's official rhetoric
To explain the Salic Law to the audience
The mock-heroic, classically-allusive style of earlier Elizabethan stage-villains (especially the Marlovian bombastic tradition), full of misused Latin and over-inflated oaths

trials.prompt: What is the actual quarrel between Pistol and Nym?
options: An eight-shilling gambling debt — and Pistol's having married Nym's betrothed Nell Quickly
